perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed, interpreted and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of Alabama, without giving effect to its conflict of laws provisions. Any dispute arising out of or in connection with this JSMA, including without limitation any question regarding its existence, validity or termination, that is not resolved in accordance with the provisions set forth below in Section 13.3, shall be submitted to the exclusive jurisdiction of the United States District Court for the Southern District of Alabama in the City of Mobile, unless that court does not have subject-matter jurisdiction or declines jurisdiction, in which case any such dispute shall be submitted to the exclusive jurisdiction of the State Court for the State of Alabama, City of Mobile, as described in the following paragraphs. Any counterclaim arising out of, or in connection with, the dispute shall be brought in the same proceeding.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed, interpreted and construed without reference to the laws of Alabama. Any dispute arising out of or in connection with this JSMA, including without limitation any question regarding its existence, validity or termination, that is not resolved in accordance with the provisions set forth below in Section 13.3, shall be submitted to arbitration.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the reference to Alabama law, the contract creates ambiguity and potential unenforceability. Omitting governing state law also removes implied consumer protections that would exist under Alabama's Uniform Commercial Code or other state consumer protection statutes. Removing reference to state or federal court would also negate the consumer's ability to seek redress in the court.
perturbation.law_explanation: Alabama's Deceptive Trade Practices Act (Ala. Code § 8-19-5) protects consumers from deceptive acts or practices in trade or commerce. By removing the reference to Alabama law, this protection is effectively removed. A consumer in Alabama would have a more difficult time arguing that the contract is subject to the DTPA and thus has less protection.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Alabama Code § 8-19-5 (2022) - Unlawful Trade Practices. :: 2022 Code of Alabama :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of this Section 2024 Code of Alabama 2023 Code of Alabama 2022 Code of Alabama (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2022 Code of Alabama Title 8 - Commercial Law and Consumer Protection. Chapter 19 - Deceptive Trade Practices. Section 8-19-5 - Unlawful Trade Practices. Universal Citation: AL Code § 8-19-5 (2022) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next Section 8-19-5 Unlawful trade practices. The following deceptive acts or practices in the conduct of any trade or commerce are hereby declared to be unlawful: (1) Passing off goods or services as those of another, provided that this section shall not prohibit the private labeling of goods or services. (2) Causing confusion or misunderstanding as to the source, sponsorship, approval, or certification of goods or services. (3) Causing confusion or misunderstanding as to the affiliation, connection, or association with, or certification by another, provided that this section shall not prohibit the private labeling of goods or services. (4) Using deceptive representations or designations of geographic origin in connection with goods or services. (5) Representing that goods or services have sponsorship, approval, characteristics, ingredients, uses, benefits, or qualities that they do not have or that a person has sponsorship, approval, status, affiliation, or connection that he or she does not have. (6) Representing that goods are original or new if they are deteriorated, reconditioned, reclaimed, used, secondhand, or altered to the point of decreasing their value or rendering the goods unfit for the ordinary purpose for which they were purchased, provided that this subdivision shall not apply to new goods which have been reconditioned, r
perturbation.justification: The modification contradicts Alabama law by removing the reference to it, which weakens consumer protections under the Alabama Deceptive Trade Practices Act (DTPA), as explained by the LLM. Scraped Law Snippet 1, Alabama Code § 8-19-5, defines unlawful trade practices. By removing Alabama law as governing, the contract makes it more difficult for a consumer to claim protection under the DTPA, creating a legal loophole, which falls under the Omission Legal Contradiction definition, where the contract omits a legally mandated consumer protection.

perturbation.original_text: Each Party waives, to the fullest extent permitted by applicable law, any right it may have to a trial by jury in respect of any proceeding relating to this JSMA.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party waives any right it may have to a trial by jury in respect of any proceeding relating to this JSMA.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'to the fullest extent permitted by applicable law', the waiver of jury trial is made absolute and unconditional. This could be problematic because in some cases, a jury trial right cannot be waived, especially where it would be unconscionable or against public policy. Alabama Constitution provides jury trial protections, and the original wording protected that right when necessary.
perturbation.law_explanation: Article I, Section 11 of the Alabama Constitution guarantees the right to a jury trial. While this right can be waived, it must be done knowingly and voluntarily. By removing the phrase 'to the fullest extent permitted by applicable law,' the contract suggests an absolute waiver, which could be interpreted as an attempt to circumvent the constitutional protection under certain circumstances.
perturbation.justification: The removal of 'to the fullest extent permitted by applicable law' from the jury trial waiver creates a potential contradiction with the Alabama Constitution. The original text acknowledged and preserved the limits on jury trial waivers as defined by law. The altered text suggests an absolute waiver, potentially conflicting with constitutional protections for jury trials. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation support the fact that this change could be interpreted as circumventing constitutional protection. Without law snippets to provide the exact text, it is hard to say definitely, but the other information indicates the change creates a conflict.

perturbation.original_text: In the event of a termination in accordance to clause 5.3 the Defaulting Party has to indemnify and hold harmless the Non-Defaulting Party for any and all losses, damages, costs etc. incurred by the Non-defaulting Party as a consequence of an Event of Default.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event of a termination in accordance to clause 5.3 the Defaulting Party may indemnify the Non-Defaulting Party for losses incurred as a direct consequence of an Event of Default.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'has to indemnify and hold harmless' to 'may indemnify' makes indemnification optional rather than mandatory. Limiting losses to only 'direct consequence' narrows the scope of indemnification. By weakening the obligation, the non-defaulting party is exposed to greater risk, which could violate implied duties of good faith and fair dealing, especially if the defaulting party's actions cause significant, but not directly linked, damages. This creates a legal risk under Alabama law, which implies a covenant of good faith.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Implied Covenant of Good Faith and Fair Dealing in Alabama
perturbation.law_explanation: Alabama law implies a covenant of good faith and fair dealing in every contract (Ala. Code § 7-1-304). By weakening the indemnification clause and making it optional, the modification could be seen as a violation of this implied covenant if the Defaulting Party refuses to indemnify the Non-Defaulting Party for losses that are clearly a consequence of the default. This is because the change allows the Defaulting Party to benefit from its breach, while leaving the Non-Defaulting Party without adequate recourse.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Section 7-1-304 - Obligation of good faith. :: 2014 Code of Alabama :: US Codes and Statutes :: US Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of the Code of Alabama 2024 Code of Alabama 2023 Code of Alabama 2022 Code of Alabama 2014 Code of Alabama (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2014 Code of Alabama Title 7 - COMMERCIAL CODE. Article 1 - GENERAL PROVISIONS. Section 7-1-304 - Obligation of good faith. AL Code § 7-1-304 (2014) What's This? Section 7-1-304 Obligation of good faith. Every contract or duty within this title imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement. (Act 2004-524, p. 1070, §1.) perturbation.justification: The modified contract weakens the indemnification clause, making it optional ('may indemnify' instead of 'has to indemnify and hold harmless') and limiting losses to 'direct consequence'. This contradicts the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing under Alabama law, as the Defaulting Party could refuse to indemnify the Non-Defaulting Party for losses resulting from the default. Scraped Law Snippet 1 (Ala. Code § 7-1-304) states, 'Every contract or duty within this title imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement.' By making indemnification optional, the modified text undermines this obligation, allowing the Defaulting Party to potentially benefit from its breach and leaving the Non-Defaulting Party without adequate recourse, thus creating a legal contradiction.
